pages.title: Welcome to Customer Data Handling Basics
pages.subtitle: Learn how everyday business habits can help protect customer and employee information from cyber risk.

video.caption: Introduction to safe customer data handling (replace later)

pages.title: Why data handling matters
pages.text: Small businesses often store or use sensitive information such as customer contact details, payment information, employee records, invoices, and account documents. This becomes a cybersecurity issue when information is shared too broadly, stored in unsafe places, left exposed on devices, or sent through insecure channels. Poor data handling can lead to unauthorized access, fraud, identity theft, customer distrust, and legal or business consequences.

video.caption: Why safe handling of customer data matters

pages.title: What kind of data needs protection?
pages.text:
Not all business data has the same risk, but many common records should be handled carefully.

Examples include:

• Customer names, addresses, phone numbers, and email addresses
• Payment or billing information
• Employee records and payroll information
• Contracts, invoices, and account files
• Login credentials or account recovery information

If the information could harm a person, customer, employee, or business if exposed, it should be handled with care.

video.caption: Examples of sensitive business and customer data

image.alt: Examples of customer and business data

pages.title: Sensitive data and regulations
pages.text: Some types of information are protected by privacy laws and industry regulations. For example: • HIPAA protects medical and health information handled by healthcare providers. • Financial and payment data may fall under payment security standards. • Personal identifying information such as addresses, phone numbers, and account data should be handled carefully. Even if your organization is not directly regulated, protecting this information is still critical to prevent fraud, identity theft, and reputational damage. Always follow your organization's policies when storing, sharing, or accessing sensitive data.

video.caption: Overview of sensitive data regulations (replace later)

image.alt: Sensitive data and compliance illustration (replace later)

bullets.title: Data exposure does not always come from hackers
bullets.text: Sometimes information is exposed because it was emailed to the wrong person, stored on an unprotected device, or shared too broadly.

bullets.title: Employee information matters too
bullets.text: Payroll records, tax forms, and internal personnel details should be protected just like customer data.

bullets.title: Access should be limited
bullets.text: Not every employee needs access to every file. Limiting access reduces risk.

bullets.title: Simple habits can prevent major problems
bullets.text: Checking recipients, using strong passwords, and storing files in approved locations can prevent many incidents.

image.alt: Did you know customer data graphic (replace later)

pages.title: Common data handling mistakes
pages.text:
Many data incidents happen because of everyday mistakes rather than advanced attacks.

Common examples include:

• Sending sensitive files to the wrong email recipient
• Saving business files on personal devices without approval
• Sharing folders with more people than necessary
• Reusing weak passwords on business systems
• Leaving printed or digital records accessible to unauthorized people
• Storing customer data in unapproved tools or locations

Even small mistakes can create serious privacy and security risks.

image.alt: Common customer data handling mistakes (replace later)

video.caption: Example of a small business data handling mistake and its impact (replace later)

pages.title: Safe habits to follow
pages.text: To handle customer and employee data more safely: 1) Only access the data you need 2) Store files in approved business systems 3) Double-check recipients before sending emails 4) Use strong passwords and MFA where available 5) Report lost devices, mistaken sharing, or suspicious access quickly Good cybersecurity starts with careful daily habits, not just technical tools.

video.caption: Safe habits for handling customer and employee data
